title: Genesis 22
subtitle: The Binding of Isaac: the Akedah

sections.header: Verses 1–10 — The Test: Abraham Commanded to Offer Isaac

poi.name: Region of Moriah
poi.text: Moriah is identified in 2 Chronicles 3:1 as the site where Solomon built the Temple — the same hill where Abraham binds Isaac. The Akedah and the Temple worship occupy the same ground. Every subsequent sacrifice at the Jerusalem Temple occurs on the mountain where the first near-sacrifice was interrupted.

poi.name: Three-Day Journey from Beersheba
poi.text: Three days of walking from Beersheba (southern Canaan) to Moriah (central hill country) is geographically plausible. The three days create narrative suspense and the theological space for Abraham's faith to travel: "we will worship and we will return" (v.5) is spoken on the third day's approach.

tl.name: Akedah — this passage
tl.text: Three days' journey from Beersheba to Moriah. God provides at the last possible moment. The oath that follows is the most expansive covenant restatement, triggered by the most extreme obedience.

notes.note: MacArthur: 'Take your son, your only son, whom you love -- Isaac' -- the four-fold intensification of the command (son, only son, whom you love, Isaac) makes clear that God is demanding Abraham's most precious possession. The test is of the covenant's ultimate question: does Abraham love the Giver or the gift?

MacArthur: 'God himself will provide the lamb for the burnt offering, my son' -- Abraham's answer to Isaac's question is prophetic beyond what Abraham fully understood. His faith-statement becomes the theological summary of the entire sacrificial system: God himself provides the substitute. YHWH Yireh (v.14) is the name that encodes this truth.

heb.gloss: to test / to put to proof
heb.paragraph: "God tested (nissāh) Abraham" (v.1) — the same verb used at Sinai: "God has come to test (nasôt) you" (Exod 20:20; Deut 8:2). The test is not for God's information (he knows Abraham) but for Abraham's own proving and strengthening. Testing in the OT is always constructive, never punitive — it is the crucible in which covenant faith is refined. James 1:3 uses the same concept: "the testing of your faith produces endurance." The Akedah is the supreme test of the covenant relationship.

"Abraham and his son went together (yaḥdāw)" (v.6, 8) — the repeated word charges the narrative with dread and tenderness. Father and son walk together toward the sacrifice. Isaac carries the wood (on which he will be placed); Abraham carries the fire and the knife. The yaḥdāw — "together" — is both the physical description of their walk and the theological description of their shared faith-obedience. Isaac is not merely a passive victim but a willing participant — the text specifies that Isaac submitted (22:9-10 — Abraham bound him; Isaac did not resist).

heb.gloss: will see / will provide / will be seen
heb.paragraph: The name Abraham gives the mountain — YHWH Yirʾeh, "the LORD will provide/see to it" (v.14) — is a perfect play on two meanings: (1) YHWH provides (rāʾāh in the sense of foresee and supply); (2) YHWH will be seen (the theophanic presence). "On the mountain of the LORD it will be provided/seen" (v.14) — the double meaning anticipates Moriah as the temple mount where sacrifice and divine presence will converge. Abraham's naming of the mountain is prophetic: Moriah will be the site of the temple, where provision and presence meet permanently.

panels.ctx: The command to sacrifice Isaac creates a perfect theological crisis: the God who promised "through Isaac shall your offspring be named" (21:12) now commands Isaac's death. How can both be true? Hebrews 11:17–19 gives the answer: Abraham "considered that God was able even to raise him from the dead, from which, figuratively speaking, he did receive him back." Abraham's faith was resurrection faith — the only logic that could reconcile the divine promise with the divine command.

cross.note: The fullest NT interpretation: Abraham offered Isaac believing God could raise him — "figuratively speaking, he did receive him back." The Akedah is understood as a resurrection type.
Paul's language mirrors Genesis 22: "He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for all of us" — the Akedah is the OT backdrop for the atonement.
"God so loved the world that he gave his only Son" — echoes "your son, your only son, whom you love" (v.2). The divine Father re-enacts Abraham's role at Calvary without staying his hand.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The binding of Isaac (the Akedah) opens with the most devastating command in the Abraham narrative: "Take your son, your only son, whom you love — Isaac — and go to the region of Moriah." Sarna: "The fourfold identification — 'son', 'only son', 'whom you love', 'Isaac' — delays the command to its most specific and personal form. Each qualifier intensifies: not just any son, but the only son, and not just the only son but the loved son, and not just the loved son but Isaac — the son of the promise."

Moriah: Sarna identifies the region with Jerusalem — the mountain on which Solomon's temple will be built (2 Chr 3:1). The connection is not incidental: the place of the Akedah becomes the place of the temple sacrifices, which become the place of the final sacrifice. The whole sacrificial theology of Israel is geographically rooted in Abraham's act on Moriah.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Abraham rises early in the morning. Alter: "The immediate action is the first and most significant detail in the narrative. There is no recorded deliberation, no dialogue with Sarah, no prayer, no argument. The man who argued boldly with God about Sodom in chapter 18 says nothing here. He simply rises early and goes."

The boy asks, "where is the lamb?" — and Abraham answers, "God himself will provide the lamb." Alter: "The exchange is the most agonizing in the chapter. The question is entirely innocent; the questioner does not know how perfectly the question exposes the situation. And Abraham's answer — God will provide the lamb — is at once a statement of faith and a refusal to deceive. The reader cannot tell whether Abraham believes it or is simply unable to say the truth."

The test of Abraham is the supreme test of faith precisely because it requires Abraham to surrender what God gave him. Calvin: "God requires at his hands the sacrifice of his son, by whose death the covenant should be ratified. Therefore the faith of Abraham was put to the severest trial — he must sacrifice the very promise on the altar." The paradox of the test is total: God commands the destruction of the means of God's own promise.

Calvin on "God will provide the lamb": "Abraham's answer is ambiguous — he either spoke prophetically, being moved by the Spirit to say more than he knew, or he suppressed the truth to spare his son's feelings. In either case, his answer was literally true: God did provide the lamb." The ambiguity is preserved because both readings are theologically significant.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on Moriah discusses the identification with Jerusalem. The note observes that 2 Chronicles 3:1 explicitly identifies Mount Moriah as the site of Solomon's temple. The note notes that while the identification is traditional and has ancient support, the geography of Genesis 22 (a three-day journey from Beersheba) is broadly consistent with the Jerusalem location.

The NET note on "God tested Abraham" discusses whether the test was cruel given God's foreknowledge of the outcome. The note observes that the test was real — Abraham did not know the outcome — and that its purpose was the demonstration of faith to Abraham himself and to subsequent generations, not to provide information to God.

sections.header: Verses 11–19 — The Ram; the Oath of Blessing

notes.note: MacArthur: 'Now I know that you fear God, because you have not withheld from me your son, your only son' -- God's statement is not that he lacked knowledge before but that Abraham's obedience has demonstrated the reality of his faith. The test reveals what was already true; it does not create it. Faith tested is faith proved.

MacArthur: The oath sworn by God himself (v.16: 'I swear by myself') is the most solemn possible divine commitment in the OT. The Hebrews 6:13-18 commentary identifies this oath as the double guarantee of God's promise -- the word and the oath -- that gives covenant heirs absolute certainty. The cross of Christ is the event to which this oath ultimately pointed.

heb.paragraph: The ram caught in the thicket (v.13) is both the immediate provision and the typological substitute. An ʾayil (adult male sheep) replaces Isaac on the altar — the animal substituted for the son. The sacrificial system of Leviticus will institutionalise this substitution: the animal dies in place of the human, its blood providing atonement. The Christian reading (Heb 11:17-19; Rom 8:32) identifies Jesus as both the Isaac who does not ultimately die and the ram who does — the substitution that the Akedah typologically anticipates.

heb.gloss: to swear / to take an oath
heb.paragraph: "I swear by myself (bî nišbaʿtî)" (v.16) — God swears by his own name (the only being by whom he can swear, since there is nothing higher — Heb 6:13-18) because Abraham "has done this and has not withheld his son, his only son." The divine oath amplifies and seals the covenant promises. The double mention of Isaac as "your son, your only son" (vv.2, 12, 16) is the narrative's emphasis: the more costly the sacrifice offered, the more unconditional the blessing promised in return.

"Through your offspring all nations on earth will be blessed, because (ʿēqeb ʾăšer) you have obeyed me" (v.18) — the ʿēqeb ("because/on the heel of") links Abraham's obedience at Moriah to the Gentile blessing of 12:3. The universal blessing is not finally grounded in Abraham's merit but in the faithfulness demonstrated at the ultimate test. Paul reads this ("and to your seed, meaning one person, who is Christ," Gal 3:16) as the promise that finds its ultimate fulfilment in the one who did what Isaac was willing to do.

cross.note: Solomon built the temple on Mount Moriah — the same mountain where Abraham offered Isaac. The connection is explicit: the place of substitutionary sacrifice becomes the permanent place of substitutionary sacrifice.
The Suffering Servant is described as a lamb led to slaughter — the Akedah's ram becomes the prophetic image of vicarious atonement. "The LORD has laid on him the iniquity of us all."
John the Baptist: "Behold, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world" — the ultimate fulfillment of "God will provide for himself the lamb."

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The ram caught in the thicket — God's provision — gives the site its name: YHWH Yireh ("the LORD will see/provide"). Sarna: "The name plays on the earlier narrative: God saw Abraham's fear (v.12), Abraham called the place 'the LORD sees', and the proverb arises 'on the mountain of the LORD it will be seen/provided.' The provision is also a seeing — God sees the need and provides."

The oath that follows the Akedah is the most solemn statement of the Abrahamic covenant in the entire narrative. Sarna: "The divine oath — 'I swear by myself' — is unique in the Abraham narrative. God swears by his own being because there is nothing greater to swear by (Heb 6:13). The oath is a response to Abraham's obedience: the covenant terms are now confirmed with the highest possible divine commitment."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The ram caught by its horns in the thicket. Alter: "The ram appears with magnificent narrative economy: no explanation of how it came to be there, no divine announcement, no fanfare. 'Abraham looked up and there in a thicket he saw a ram.' The provision comes without explanation, just as the command came without explanation. The narrative trusts the reader to register the theological weight of the provision."

The divine oath is the narrative climax of the entire Abraham story. Alter: "The oath is sworn by God's own being — the highest possible guarantee. The covenant promises that have been stated and restated through the story are now confirmed by an oath that cannot be revoked. Abraham's obedience has not earned the promises; it has confirmed the covenant's reliability. The oath is the divine seal on what was already promised."

The angel's declaration — "now I know that you fear God" — is not a discovery for God but a public demonstration. Calvin: "God says 'now I know' not as if he had been previously ignorant, but because the thing which he had proved in the hidden recesses of Abraham's heart is now made manifest. The test brought to light what was already real."

The divine oath on the basis of Abraham's obedience does not make the covenant conditional on human merit. Calvin: "God swears by himself, not by Abraham's merit, but because Abraham's obedience is the occasion — not the cause — of the oath. The cause is in God's own gracious will. The obedience provided the occasion for the oath to be given in its most final and irrevocable form."

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on YHWH Yireh discusses whether yireh means "will see" or "will provide." The note observes that the Hebrew root raah (to see) develops the meaning "to provide" through the connection between seeing a need and meeting it. The note translates "the LORD will provide" while noting the visual dimension of the original. The proverb — "on the mountain of the LORD it will be provided" — anticipates the temple theology of Israel.

The NET note on the second divine speech observes this is the only place in the Abraham narrative where God swears an oath — the ultimate divine self-commitment. The second speech confirms the covenant with the most solemn possible divine declaration.

MacArthur: The genealogy of Nahor, Abraham's brother, introduces Rebekah (v.23) -- the future wife of Isaac. The information comes immediately after the Akedah and immediately before the death of Sarah (ch.23). The covenant line is being prepared for its next generation before the reader is aware of the need.

MacArthur: The family tree of Nahor signals the narrator's forward planning. Rebekah appears in v.23 before the death of Sarah in ch.23 or the search for Isaac's wife in ch.24. The covenant God is already preparing the next generation's bride while the Akedah's altar fire is still warm.

heb.paragraph: The genealogy of Nahor's family (vv.20-24) seems anticlimactic after the Akedah — but is narratively essential. Rebekah (v.23) is introduced here, two chapters before she appears in the story. The genealogy is prospective — it places the next covenant woman within Abraham's extended family before the narrative needs her, ensuring that Isaac's wife will be from within the covenant lineage. The placement after the Akedah suggests that Abraham's obedience opens the way for the next stage of covenant history: the next generation's marriage and continuation.

The name Rebekah is listed in v.23 without comment, her role still future. The etymology is uncertain (possibly "secured/tied" or "flattering/seductive"). Her eventual characterisation — energetic, decisive, strategically minded, and deeply concerned with the covenant birthright — will make her the most active female character in the patriarchal narratives. The genealogy plants her here as a seed: she is the providential answer to the prayer of Isaac (24:67: "Isaac... was comforted after his mother's death").

The covenant promise in v.17 — "I will make your descendants (zarʿakā) as numerous as the stars in the sky and as the sand on the seashore. Your descendants will take possession of the cities of their enemies" — uses zera (seed/offspring) three times in two verses. Paul's reading of "seed" as singular in Gal 3:16 ("he does not say 'and to seeds,' meaning many people, but 'and to your seed,' meaning one person, who is Christ") finds the singular use of zera pointing beyond the collective to the one Seed who fulfils all the promises.

cross.note: God swore by himself because there was no greater — the divine oath that followed the Akedah becomes the "anchor for the soul" that grounds Christian hope in "two unchangeable things" (promise + oath).
Paul reads "your offspring" as pointing to Christ (singular): "It does not say, 'And to offsprings,' referring to many, but referring to one, 'And to your offspring,' who is Christ."

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The genealogy of Nahor's family bridges the Abraham narrative to the Isaac narrative. Sarna: "Rebekah appears here for the first time — the wife Isaac will marry in chapter 24. The placement of the genealogy immediately after the Akedah is deliberate: having demonstrated his willingness to sacrifice the son of the promise, Abraham receives news that the covenant line's continuation is already being prepared."

Sarna notes the arrival of twelve nations through Nahor parallels the twelve princes of Ishmael (25:12-16) and anticipates the twelve tribes of Israel (35:22-26). The three-branch structure is the Table of Nations compressed into covenant history.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The genealogy is a narrative bridge — linking the Akedah to the marriage of Isaac. Alter: "The genealogy arrives without transition or explanation. After the height of the Akedah, the narrative descends immediately to a family list. The effect is characteristic of biblical narrative: the sublime and the prosaic occupy the same pages. The genealogy reminds us that the covenant works through ordinary family life as well as through extraordinary tests."

Rebekah appears at the end of a genealogical list without emphasis. Alter notes the narrative strategy: she is introduced without significance so her emergence as the central figure of the next cycle will be achieved through story, not genealogical announcement.

Calvin on the timing of the genealogy: "This narration of Moses is seasonably placed here: after so great a trial, God does not leave Abraham without consolation. He shows him that the race of his brother, who went before him into a foreign land, had greatly increased — a sign that the promises of God were not in vain."

Calvin: God begins to provide for the covenant's succession even before the patriarch knows of the need. The genealogy arrives — providentially — at the moment when Sarah's approaching death will soon require Isaac to have a wife.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on Rebekah observes that her name appears here without emphasis — she is simply one of eight children of Bethuel. The note observes that the narrative technique is typical of Genesis: future major characters are introduced in minor roles before the narrative focuses on them.

The NET note on Rebekah's genealogical introduction observes her elaborated family descriptors — 'these eight Milcah bore to Nahor, Abraham's brother' and 'Rebekah, the sister of Laban' — anticipate the marriage narrative's key relationships.

ppl.name: Abraham
ppl.text: At the apex of his faith journey. He "considers" God able to raise Isaac (Heb 11:19), walks the three-day journey, raises the knife. His faith is declared righteous not in the easy moments but at the hardest point. The NT consistently returns to this moment as the definition of covenant faithfulness.

ppl.name: Isaac
ppl.text: Old enough to carry the wood (v.6), strong enough to be bound (v.9). Patristic tradition emphasizes Isaac's willing submission as a type of Christ's willing sacrifice. The text's silence about Isaac's inner response is filled by centuries of interpretation.

ppl.name: The RAM
ppl.text: The anonymous ram provides the substitutionary sacrifice. In the economy of the Akedah, the ram's death enables Isaac's life. This is the logic of atonement: one dies that another might live. The ram points forward to the Lamb of God.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"Abraham answered, 'God himself will provide the lamb for the burnt offering, my son.' And the two of them went on together."ESV"Abraham said, 'God will provide for himself the lamb for a burnt offering, my son.' So they went both of them together."KJV"And Abraham said, My son, God will provide himself a lamb for a burnt offering: so they went both of them together."NASB"Abraham said, 'God will provide for Himself the lamb for the burnt offering, my son.' So the two of them walked on together."

src.title: Aqedah in Jewish Tradition
src.quote: The Akedah became central to Jewish theology of merit and intercession. In the Midrash, God's mercy toward Israel is repeatedly grounded in "the merit of the Akedah" — Abraham's willingness to sacrifice Isaac creates a permanent fund of divine favor. The shofar (ram's horn) blown at Rosh Hashanah recalls the ram of Genesis 22.

src.title: Dead Sea Scrolls 4Q225 (Pseudo-Jubilees)
src.quote: A fragmentary Qumran text dramatically expands the Akedah, introducing Mastema (the adversary figure) as the one who urged God to test Abraham — paralleling Job 1 and anticipating Satan's role in the NT.

rec.text: Kierkegaard's masterpiece uses the Akedah to explore the "teleological suspension of the ethical" — Abraham's obedience transcends ordinary moral categories. His "Knight of Faith" is defined by the ability to hold the paradox: command and promise simultaneously. The book is one of the most influential philosophical treatments of biblical faith.

Rashi's influential commentary emphasizes that God "tested" Abraham to demonstrate his righteousness to the world — God knew the outcome. He also notes that the angel stopped Abraham before the sacrifice (not the LORD himself) as a sign that God never truly intended Isaac's death.

The Fathers uniformly read the Akedah typologically: Isaac carrying the wood = Christ carrying the cross; the three-day journey = resurrection on the third day; the ram caught in thorns = Christ crowned with thorns; Mount Moriah = Calvary. The typological reading is one of the most elaborate in all of biblical interpretation.

rows.label: The Akedah: Maximum Surrender, Maximum Provision
rows.text: Three-day journey, the bound son, the raised knife -- then the ram. God provides at the moment of maximum surrender, not before. YHWH Yireh names the God who provides at the point of complete dependence.

rows.label: The Oath After Obedience
rows.text: The only divine oath in the Abraham narrative follows the Akedah. Because you have done this -- the most demanding act occasions the most expansive restatement of the promise. Obedience does not earn the blessing; it opens the space for its fullest expression.

hebtext.gloss: to test / to put to proof
hebtext.note: "God tested (nissāh) Abraham" (v.1) — the same verb used at Sinai: "God has come to test (nasôt) you" (Exod 20:20; Deut 8:2). The test is not for God's information (he knows Abraham) but for Abraham's own proving and strengthening. Testing in the OT is always constructive, never punitive — it is the crucible in which covenant faith is refined. James 1:3 uses the same concept: "the testing of your faith produces endurance." The Akedah is the supreme test of the covenant relationship.

"Abraham and his son went together (yaḥdāw)" (v.6, 8) — the repeated word charges the narrative with dread and tenderness. Father and son walk together toward the sacrifice. Isaac carries the wood (on which he will be placed); Abraham carries the fire and the knife. The yaḥdāw — "together" — is both the physical description of their walk and the theological description of their shared faith-obedience. Isaac is not merely a passive victim but a willing participant — the text specifies that Isaac submitted (22:9-10 — Abraham bound him; Isaac did not resist).

hebtext.gloss: will see / will provide / will be seen
hebtext.note: The name Abraham gives the mountain — YHWH Yirʾeh, "the LORD will provide/see to it" (v.14) — is a perfect play on two meanings: (1) YHWH provides (rāʾāh in the sense of foresee and supply); (2) YHWH will be seen (the theophanic presence). "On the mountain of the LORD it will be provided/seen" (v.14) — the double meaning anticipates Moriah as the temple mount where sacrifice and divine presence will converge. Abraham's naming of the mountain is prophetic: Moriah will be the site of the temple, where provision and presence meet permanently.

The ram caught in the thicket (v.13) is both the immediate provision and the typological substitute. An ʾayil (adult male sheep) replaces Isaac on the altar — the animal substituted for the son. The sacrificial system of Leviticus will institutionalise this substitution: the animal dies in place of the human, its blood providing atonement. The Christian reading (Heb 11:17-19; Rom 8:32) identifies Jesus as both the Isaac who does not ultimately die and the ram who does — the substitution that the Akedah typologically anticipates.

hebtext.gloss: to swear / to take an oath
hebtext.note: "I swear by myself (bî nišbaʿtî)" (v.16) — God swears by his own name (the only being by whom he can swear, since there is nothing higher — Heb 6:13-18) because Abraham "has done this and has not withheld his son, his only son." The double mention of Isaac as "your son, your only son" (vv.2, 12, 16) is the narrative's emphasis: the more costly the sacrifice offered, the more unconditional the blessing promised in return.

"Through your offspring all nations on earth will be blessed, because (ʿēqeb ʾăšer) you have obeyed me" (v.18) — the ʿēqeb ("because/on the heel of") links Abraham's obedience at Moriah to the Gentile blessing of 12:3. Paul reads this ("and to your seed, meaning one person, who is Christ," Gal 3:16) as the promise that finds its ultimate fulfilment in the one who did what Isaac was willing to do.

The genealogy of Nahor's family (vv.20-24) seems anticlimactic after the Akedah — but is narratively essential. Rebekah (v.23) is introduced here, two chapters before she appears in the story. The genealogy is prospective — it places the next covenant woman within Abraham's extended family before the narrative needs her, ensuring that Isaac's wife will be from within the covenant lineage. The placement after the Akedah suggests that Abraham's obedience opens the way for the next stage of covenant history: the next generation's marriage and continuation.

The name Rebekah is listed in v.23 without comment, her role still future. The etymology is uncertain (possibly "secured/tied" or "flattering/seductive"). Her eventual characterisation — energetic, decisive, strategically minded, and deeply concerned with the covenant birthright — will make her the most active female character in the patriarchal narratives. The genealogy plants her here as a seed: she is the providential answer to the prayer of Isaac (24:67: "Isaac... was comforted after his mother's death").

The covenant promise in v.17 — "I will make your descendants (zarʿakā) as numerous as the stars in the sky and as the sand on the seashore. Your descendants will take possession of the cities of their enemies" — uses zera (seed/offspring) three times in two verses. Paul's reading of "seed" as singular in Gal 3:16 ("he does not say 'and to seeds,' meaning many people, but 'and to your seed,' meaning one person, who is Christ") finds the singular use of zera pointing beyond the collective to the one Seed who fulfils all the promises.

tx.title: Moriah — Gerizim in the Samaritan tradition
tx.content: MT identifies the binding site as 'the land of Moriah,' linked to the Jerusalem temple mount in 2 Chr 3:1. The SP does not alter this verse directly, but the broader Samaritan tradition identifies the site with Mount Gerizim rather than Moriah/Zion. The LXX translates as τὴν γῆν τὴν ὑψηλήν ('the high land'), avoiding the place name.
tx.note: This is one of the most theologically charged geographic identifications in the Bible. The LXX's vague rendering may reflect an attempt to sidestep the Jerusalem–Gerizim rivalry.

tx.title: Ram caught in the thicket — 'one ram' variant
tx.content: MT reads 'a ram behind, caught in a thicket by its horns' (ʾayil ʾaḥar). Some LXX manuscripts read 'one ram' (eis), and others omit 'behind' entirely. The difficulty is ʾaḥar — does it mean 'behind (him)' or 'one/another'? The SP reads ʾeḥād ('one'), resolving the ambiguity.
tx.note: The SP's ʾeḥād is likely a clarifying correction of a genuinely ambiguous MT. The resulting meaning ('behold, one ram, caught…') is smoother but possibly secondary.

debate.title: The Akedah as Polemic Against Child Sacrifice

positions.name: Anti-sacrifice polemic
positions.argument: The story's primary purpose is to establish that YHWH, unlike Molech, does not require child sacrifice. The near-sacrifice is the test; the ram-substitution is the theological conclusion: God provides a substitute.

positions.name: Test of faith
positions.argument: Hebrews 11:17-19 reads the Akedah as a test of resurrection faith: Abraham believed God could raise Isaac. The anti-sacrifice polemic may be a secondary function; the primary meaning is the testing and vindicating of Abrahamic faith.

positions.name: Typological/messianic
positions.argument: The ram as substitute, the father-son relationship, the wood, the mountain, the three-day journey — all point forward to the cross. The Akedah is the most developed Christological type in Genesis.
